title: Cross-Entropy Is All You Need To Invert the Data Generating Process

reviews.summary: This paper develops an identifiability theory for cross-entropy-based learning under a cluster-centric data generating process (DGP). Starting from a von Mises-Fisher (vMF) latent model with class “cluster vectors,” the authors prove that parametric instance discrimination (DIET) recovers latent variables and cluster vectors up to a linear (orthogonal/affine) transform, and then extend the result to standard supervised classification with cross-entropy. Empirical studies on synthetic data (matching assumptions), disentanglement benchmarks (DisLib), and ImageNet-X show that features learned with cross-entropy permit strong linear decoding of latent/proxy factors.
strengths: Extends nonlinear ICA-style identifiability to a parametric instance discrimination (PID) setup with an explicit, cluster-centric vMF DGP, including both normalized and unnormalized representations.

Bridges identifiability results in SSL (GCL/TCL/InfoNCE) to supervised learning by proving that cross-entropy classification can recover ground-truth factors up to a linear transform under the proposed DGP; the genealogy framing is conceptually unifying.
Identifies conditions under which the classifier head rows collapse to class cluster vectors, and links the encoder’s latent variables to ground truth via a shared orthogonal/affine map.
Synthetic experiments closely match theoretical assumptions and provide near-perfect linear recovery (R² ≈ 1) across a range of dimensions, numbers of clusters, dataset sizes, and concentrations.
Stress tests with misspecified conditionals (Laplace/Normal) still show high linear recoverability, suggesting empirical robustness of the identifiability phenomenon beyond the vMF assumption.
DisLib experiments and ImageNet-X linear probing demonstrate that cross-entropy-trained representations linearly encode salient factors in controlled and naturalistic settings.
Clear statement of DIET objective and the DGP; helpful intuition for why rows of W align with cluster vectors and why embeddings align with latent z.
The genealogy figure/treatment effectively situates the contribution relative to GCL/TCL/InfoNCE and clarifies how supervised learning emerges as a special case.
Offers a plausible formal explanation for widely observed phenomena (linear decodability, transfer with linear probes, convergence of representations/superposition).

If accepted, could influence how the community reasons about when and why supervised cross-entropy yields linearly structured, interpretable features, bridging SSL identifiability and practical supervised pipelines.
weaknesses: The identifiability guarantees rely on a strong and specific DGP (vMF on a hypersphere with sufficiently “large and spread out” cluster vectors; injective generator; global minimization), and some assumptions are informal (e.g., what “sufficiently large and spread out” entails).

The supervised learning result (Theorem 2) is stated concisely and somewhat unusually (“h = f ∘ g is a linear map from S^(d-1) to R^d”); the precise assumptions (Assumption 3) and proof mechanics need more transparency for readers to assess generality and edge cases.

The transition from instance-level heads (N rows) to class-level heads (|C| rows) is nontrivial; the paper asserts equality of rows for the same class at optimum, but the conditions ensuring this collapse are not fully spelled out.

DisLib and ImageNet-X results show linear decodability, not full identifiability up to a linear transform; a direct linear alignment test (e.g., Procrustes) between learned features and known factors would strengthen the empirical claim.
Missing comparisons to strong SSL baselines (InfoNCE/SimCLR/MoCo) trained under similar regimes to test the “cross-entropy is sufficient” claim against alternatives.

Real-data results are modest in effect size for many factors (ImageNet-X accuracies often ~0.60), and proxy labels complicate causal interpretation as true latents; broader benchmarking or ablations (e.g., label noise, data imbalance) would contextualize robustness.

Some assumptions and theorem statements are given in “simplified” form; key technical conditions (e.g., exact distributional separations, capacity/regularization requirements, optimization landscape) should be specified in the main text, not only appendices.
The supervised theorem’s conclusion of linearity on the sphere may be counterintuitive; more intuition or an outline of the proof is needed to avoid overinterpretation.
Limited discussion of supervised identifiability or theory beyond ICA (e.g., connections to information geometry, Bayes-optimal linearity under specific priors, margin/separability analyses).
Empirical comparisons to identifiable disentanglement methods (e.g., β-VAE/FactorVAE variants; weakly supervised disentanglement) are absent, despite conceptual overlap.
reviews.questions:
1. Theorem 2 states that h = f ∘ g is a linear map from S^(d-1) to R^d. Could you restate the full assumptions (Assumption 3) and provide a proof sketch in the main text? In particular, is the linearity global (i.e., extends to a linear operator on R^d), and how does it reconcile with nonlinearity off the sphere?
2. What are the precise, checkable conditions behind “the system {v_c} is sufficiently large and spread out”? Can you quantify required angular separations, cluster count relative to d, or concentration κ for the identifiability to hold?
3. Under what conditions do the DIET rows collapse to identical vectors for samples of the same class (w_i = w_j if C(i)=C(j))? Is additional regularization (e.g., row normalization, temperature β, weight decay) required, or does the result hold for any global minimizer?
4. How sensitive are the supervised results to label noise or class imbalance? Since cross-entropy is known to be robust up to a point, does identifiability degrade gracefully with controlled noise (e.g., symmetric/asymmetric flips)?
5. Can you provide a direct alignment analysis on DisLib (e.g., Procrustes to match learned subspaces with true continuous factors, including permutation/scale ambiguities) to more tightly mirror the identifiability claim rather than only reporting correlations?
6. Could you include a direct comparison to an InfoNCE baseline (with and without projector discard) and a standard β-VAE/FactorVAE on DisLib to contextualize performance and linear identifiability?
7. For non-Euclidean factors (e.g., rotations), do you expect identifiable subspaces (e.g., 2D sin/cos planes) under the proposed DGP? If not, what minimal extensions to the DGP/theory would accommodate group-structured latent manifolds?
8. Does your theory imply anything about the geometry of class prototypes learned in practice (e.g., approximate vMF structure on the penultimate layer)? Could you test this on ImageNet by fitting vMFs per class?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents a compelling and timely attempt to unify identifiability results across SSL and supervised learning by proving that cross-entropy minimization in a cluster-centric vMF DGP yields linearly identifiable representations. The DIET analysis is technically interesting and well-supported by synthetic experiments; the extension to supervised classification is particularly impactful if the assumptions and consequences (linearity of h on the sphere) are made more explicit and convincing. The empirical evidence on DisLib and ImageNet-X supports the central narrative of linear decodability, although it falls short of an exhaustive validation of identifiability in natural data. I view the work as a meaningful conceptual advance that will interest the ICLR community, with room to strengthen clarity (assumptions and proofs in the main text), add baselines, and tighten empirical alignment with the theoretical claims. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifications around Theorem 2 and expanded empirical comparisons.
